label: The Twelve — Part 3 — Ovadyah, Yonah, Mikhah, Nakhum
summary_plain: Ovadyah prophesies against Edom — you who dwell in the clefts of the rock, whose habitation is high, who say in your heart: Who will bring me down? YHWH will bring you down. Because you stood aside on the day your brother was carried away captive. Yonah is sent to Nineveh — he flees, is swallowed by a great fish, spends three days and three nights in its belly, is vomited out, and goes to Nineveh. The city repents. YHWH relents. Yonah is angry. Mikhah declares what YHWH requires: Do justice, love mercy, walk humbly with your Creator. He prophesies: from you, Bet Lechem Efratah, shall come one who will be ruler in Yisra'EL — whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days. Nakhum prophesies the fall of Nineveh — the lion's den, the bloody city full of lies and plunder.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from the Minor Prophets Scroll (4QXII) and the Orit Ge'ez. Ovadyah — the shortest book in the Hebrew canon — targets Edom specifically for complicity during Yerushalayim's fall: they stood by, looted, and handed over fugitives. Yonah is unique among the prophetic books — a narrative about a prophet rather than a collection of oracles. The three days in the fish's belly is the only Hebrew prophetic text explicitly cited in the NT as a typological sign (Matthew 12:40). Mikhah 6:8 is the most concise summary of covenant ethics in the entire prophetic literature. Mikhah 5:2 (Hebrew 5:1) — the Bet Lechem prophecy — is cited in Matthew 2:6 and was known at Qumran. Nakhum's oracle against Nineveh is confirmed by the archaeological record: Nineveh fell to the Babylonians and Medes in 612 BCE.

key_terms.definition: Prophet against Edom. His name means 'servant of YAH.' His book — the shortest in the Hebrew canon — is a single oracle of judgment against Edom for complicity in Yerushalayim's destruction. Anglicized as Obadiah.
Prophet sent to Nineveh who fled on a ship to Tarshish. Swallowed by a great fish for three days and three nights, then vomited out. His name means 'dove.' Anglicized as Jonah.
Prophet from Moreshet in the Shephelah. He declares what YHWH requires — justice, mercy, humility — and prophesies the ruler from Bet Lechem whose origin is from ancient days. Anglicized as Micah.

Prophet against Nineveh, the Assyrian capital. His name means 'comfort.' His oracle describes Nineveh's fall — which occurred in 612 BCE, confirmed by Babylonian chronicles and archaeology. Anglicized as Nahum.

fill_blank.prompt: He has told you, O man, what is good — and what does YHWH require of you but to do justice, and to love ______, and to walk humbly with your Creator?
fill_blank.source_quote: He has told you, O man, what is good — and what does YHWH require of you but to do justice, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with your Creator?

fill_blank.prompt: And YHWH appointed a great fish to swallow up Yonah. And Yonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: And YHWH appointed a great fish to swallow up Yonah. And Yonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.

fill_blank.prompt: But you, Bet Lechem Efratah, though you are small among the clans of Yehudah — from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ______ in Yisra'EL.
fill_blank.source_quote: But you, Bet Lechem Efratah, who are too little to be among the clans of Yehudah — from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Yisra'EL, whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days.

multiple_choice.question: What does YHWH require according to Mikhah?
options: Do justice, love mercy, walk humbly with your Creator
Fast and pray three times daily
multiple_choice.source_quote: What does YHWH require of you but to do justice, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with your Creator?

multiple_choice.question: Why is YHWH's judgment against Edom?
options: For standing aside when Yerushalayim was captured and looting the city
For refusing to trade with Yisra'EL
multiple_choice.source_quote: Because of the violence done to your brother Ya'aqov, shame shall cover you, and you shall be cut off forever. On the day that you stood aside — on the day that strangers carried off his wealth — you were like one of them.

multiple_choice.question: What happened when Nineveh repented?
options: YHWH relented from the disaster he had planned
The king of Nineveh attacked Yisra'EL
multiple_choice.source_quote: When The Creator saw what they did, how they turned from their evil way, The Creator relented of the disaster that he had said he would do to them, and he did not do it.

faq.question: How does the Great Eraser use Mikhah 6:8 while ignoring its covenant context?
faq.answer: Mikhah 6:8 — 'do justice, love mercy, walk humbly with your Creator' — is one of the most quoted verses in Western religious culture. It appears on church walls, seminary brochures, and inspirational merchandise. But the passage is not a generic ethical maxim. It is YHWH's direct answer to a covenant lawsuit (riv) that begins in 6:1: 'Hear what YHWH says — arise, plead your case before the mountains.' YHWH recounts what he has done for his people: brought them from Mitsrayim, sent Moshe, Aharon, and Miriam, turned Balak's curse into Bil'am's blessing (6:4-5). Then he asks what they have offered in return. The people propose sacrifices — thousands of rams, ten thousands of rivers of oil, even the firstborn (6:6-7). YHWH rejects all of it. What he requires is covenantal: justice, chesed (covenant-faithfulness, not generic kindness), and humble walking with YHWH. The Great Eraser extracts the ethics and removes the covenant framework. Justice becomes social niceness. Chesed becomes generic mercy. Walking humbly becomes personal piety. The covenant lawsuit — YHWH's case against his people for failing to live up to what he did for them — disappears.

faq.question: What does Ovadyah's oracle reveal about complicity in the scattering?
faq.answer: Ovadyah condemns Edom not for attacking Yerushalayim directly but for complicity — standing aside, watching, looting, and handing over fugitives (verses 11-14). Edom is Ya'aqov's brother — the descendants of Esav. The judgment is specifically for what was done to a brother: 'Because of the violence done to your brother Ya'aqov, shame shall cover you' (verse 10). The principle extends: every people, nation, and institution that stood aside during the scattering of the covenant people — that profited from the destruction without directly causing it — falls under the same oracle. The European nations that profited from the slave trade without conducting the raids. The religious institutions that theologized the enslavement without wielding the whip. The academic establishments that benefited from stolen labor without owning plantations. Ovadyah's oracle is against complicity, not just direct action. Standing aside is the charge. Edom's silence was Edom's guilt.

faq.question: What does Yonah's reluctance reveal about prophetic resistance?
faq.answer: Yonah is the only prophet who actively flees from YHWH's commission. He does not want Nineveh to repent — because Nineveh is the capital of the Assyrian empire, the power that destroyed the northern kingdom and deported its population. When Nineveh does repent and YHWH relents, Yonah is angry (4:1). He knew YHWH would do this because YHWH is gracious, merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love (4:2). The book ends with YHWH's question: should I not have compassion on Nineveh, that great city? The prophetic tradition does not give the covenant people veto power over YHWH's mercy. YHWH's justice and mercy extend to the nations — including the oppressor — when they turn. This is not Western universalism. It is YHWH's sovereign freedom to extend mercy where he chooses.
